data.title: The United States has frozen $344 million in Iranian cryptocurrency.
data.description: The US has taken action against Iran again. According to reports from CCTV News and other media outlets, on April 24th local time, the US Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), frequently mentioned in international financial sanctions news, released a new round of sanctions against Iran and simultaneously updated its "Specially Designated Nationals List." Notably, this time the US is not only targeting institutions, accounts, and transaction networks within the traditional financial system, but also including multiple cryptocurrency wallet addresses linked to Iran in the sanctions, involving approximately $344 million in frozen cryptocurrency assets. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/mmbiz_jpg/iaEv22TKgUOIib9BUjFNhxHbwGspichdQtvzonHDxsvse13fxI9U62dlv1EK03LUIqKlNyDd3QSeAup cficLlu0cwklIUxga4ibtSrcwTkJvQ9W4/640?wx_fmt=jpeg&tp=wxpic&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1&watermark=1#imgIndex=0" class="" _="" alt="image" data-before-load-time="1777088893809" data-report-img-idx="0" Reuters also reported that US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated on social media that the US Treasury Department is sanctioning multiple wallets linked to Iran and has frozen $344 million in cryptocurrency assets. More crucial information comes from Tether's own announcement. According to Tether, it cooperated with OFAC and US law enforcement agencies to freeze over $344 million in USDT from two addresses. The announcement also mentioned that these addresses were identified after US law enforcement agencies provided relevant information, and the freezing action was to prevent further transfer of funds. Therefore, simply stopping at "the US freezes Iranian cryptocurrency assets" is insufficient. A deeper understanding should be that this represents the US extending its already mature sanctions capabilities in the offline financial system to the on-chain world. Previously, the US froze bank accounts; now it's freezing on-chain addresses. Previously, the US cut off dollar clearing; now it's also cutting off stablecoin liquidity. A Set of Invisible Financial Controls. In the past, when we talked about America's global financial influence, we often didn't focus on how many warships it sent or how many statements it issued, but rather on the powerful set of financial tools it possessed. The dollar clearing system, the international banking network, the SWIFT communication system, the OFAC sanctions list, and the compliance obligations of US financial institutions—these things combined constitute America's truly formidable financial control. A country, a company, or even an individual whose capital flows are deeply dependent on the dollar system will find it difficult to completely circumvent these rules. Previously, these sanctions were more readily apparent. Bank accounts are frozen, dollar settlements are cut off, companies are placed on sanctions lists, and financial institutions dare not provide services. Even if the transaction occurs outside the United States, as long as it involves dollars, US financial institutions, or even if the relevant institutions fear being affected by secondary US sanctions, the US has ways to make it difficult for that money to move. This is why many countries, after being sanctioned by the US, have tried to find channels outside the dollar system. Cryptocurrency is not the safe haven it seems. Cryptocurrency was once understood by many as one possible path, and this logic is not difficult to understand. On-chain transfers do not require banks, do not go through traditional clearing systems, and do not go through SWIFT. As long as there is a wallet address and private key, theoretically, the transfer can be completed. Therefore, in the past few years, both sanctioned countries and some gray and black market funds have tried to use crypto assets to transfer value. But this incident shows that things are not so simple. Blockchain is not a parallel universe completely detached from the real-world financial order. Stablecoins, in particular, while circulating on the blockchain, still heavily rely on centralized institutions for their issuance, reserve, redemption, compliance, and freezing mechanisms. Many people, when discussing "cryptocurrencies," lump Bitcoin, Ethereum, USDT, and USDC together. While this is acceptable in casual conversation, their legal and power structures are vastly different. Bitcoin is truly closer to a decentralized asset. It is not issued by a company, has no single manager, and no entity that can directly press the "freeze button" upon receiving a notification from law enforcement. As long as the user possesses the private key, there is no centralized institution within the Bitcoin network that can directly freeze your account. Of course, this does not mean that Bitcoin is completely unenforceable in the real world. Law enforcement agencies can still track and dispose of Bitcoin through exchanges, custodians, OTC merchants, on-chain analytics, and judicial seizures. However, from a protocol perspective, Bitcoin itself has no single issuer that can unilaterally freeze BTC in an address. This is completely different from stablecoins. Mainstream stablecoins like USDT and USDC are essentially on-chain dollar certificates issued by centralized institutions. They circulate on-chain and appear similar to other crypto assets, but they are backed by issuing companies, reserve assets, bank accounts, compliance teams, and face regulatory pressure. From their inception, stablecoins have not been purely decentralized assets. The Dual Nature of Stablecoins Because of this, stablecoins have a very pronounced dual nature. On the one hand, it is indeed faster and cheaper than traditional bank transfers, and more suitable for cross-border flows. Especially in many regions with underdeveloped banking systems, difficulty in opening USD accounts, and high costs for cross-border remittances, stablecoins have already effectively taken on some of the functions of a "digital dollar." Many ordinary users use USDT not because they understand blockchain so well, but because it is indeed convenient, has good liquidity, fast settlement, and many transaction scenarios. On the other hand, stablecoins are not decentralized assets like Bitcoin, which have no issuer. Issuers can cooperate with law enforcement agencies to freeze addresses and restrict the continued transfer of funds. Tether's announcement has made it very clear that once a wallet is identified as being involved in sanctions evasion, criminal networks, or other illegal activities, the issuer can take restrictive measures. This is something that many ordinary users have not truly realized. You might think you're receiving "on-chain money," but from a power structure perspective, you're actually receiving on-chain liabilities issued by a centralized company. Whether this liability can be transferred often depends not only on whether you have the private key, but also on the relationships between the issuer, exchange, custodian, law enforcement, and regulatory bodies. A private key can control transaction signatures, but it may not be able to counter the issuer's ability to freeze transactions at the contract level, nor may it be able to counter the overall blocking of that address by centralized exchanges and compliance service providers. This is why the US encourages stablecoins. This is also why the US's attitude towards compliant stablecoins in recent years is worth observing. The US supports stablecoins for reasons such as financial innovation, payment efficiency, consolidating demand for the dollar, and promoting the development of the crypto industry. But from the perspective of the international financial order, there's a more practical layer: stablecoins allow the dollar system to extend beyond bank accounts to on-chain addresses. Previously, if you used a US dollar account, the US could influence you through the banking system; now, if you use a US dollar stablecoin, the US can still influence you through stablecoin issuers, centralized exchanges, custodians, and compliance service providers. It seems the technology has changed, the account format has changed, and wallet addresses have replaced bank accounts, but the underlying control logic hasn't completely changed. The US isn't simply against cryptocurrencies. On the contrary, the US is increasingly aware that there are two types of things in the crypto asset world: one is decentralized assets like Bitcoin that are truly difficult to control from a single point of contact; the other is stablecoins and centralized crypto services that can be incorporated into compliance frameworks, cooperate with law enforcement, and be absorbed by the financial sanctions system. The US may not reject the latter; it might even encourage its compliant development. The reason is also very practical: as long as stablecoins remain pegged to the US dollar, are still issued by regulated centralized institutions, and still need to cooperate with OFAC, FinCEN, the Department of Justice, and other law enforcement agencies, then they are not a substitute for the dollar system, but rather a new interface to it. Previously, the US dollar circulated within the banking system; now it can also circulate on public blockchains. Previously, the US exerted influence through banks, clearing houses, and SWIFT; now it can also exert influence through stablecoin issuers, centralized exchanges, on-chain analytics companies, and compliance service providers. On the surface, the financial system has become more open, but from a control perspective, the core issues haven't disappeared; they've simply been expressed in a different technological form. A Reminder for Crypto Industry Professionals This matter also serves as a direct reminder for those working in the crypto industry. If you are an exchange, wallet, payment company, custodian, market maker, or any Web3 financial service provider involved in stablecoin transactions, you can no longer simply explain your business as "I'm just a technology platform" or "I'm just an on-chain tool." As long as your business involves stablecoins, especially USD stablecoins, you are within the scope of global sanctions and compliance systems. Traditional financial institutions used to conduct KYC, AML, and sanctions list screenings; now many Web3 institutions cannot circumvent these procedures either, only the screening targets have changed from bank accounts to wallet addresses, and the remittance paths to on-chain fund flows. This is also a very real issue for entrepreneurs. Many projects like to talk about Web3, decentralization, and on-chain finance. But if you look at their business structure, the settlement assets are USDT, customer deposits and withdrawals rely on centralized exchanges, custody relies on centralized institutions, and risk control relies on third-party on-chain analytics companies. From a legal and regulatory perspective, this project may not be a purely decentralized project, but rather more like a traditional financial service with a new on-chain interface. Regulators aren't looking at your slogans, but rather how your funds flow, who your customers are, who controls the assets, and who bears the risks. If you're doing payments, you'll face anti-money laundering and sanctions screening; if you're doing custody, you'll face asset freezing and law enforcement assistance; if you're doing trading, you'll face KYC, KYT, and suspicious transaction identification; if you're doing stablecoin-related business, you can't avoid issues of issuers, reserve assets, redemption mechanisms, blacklisting mechanisms, and judicial assistance. For ordinary users, this also serves as a simple reminder: USDT is not Bitcoin. Many people buy USDT simply because they find it convenient, stable, and liquid. This assessment is correct; USDT does indeed play a very important liquidity role in the global crypto market. However, it would be a mistake to understand USDT as an asset that is completely uncryable, unaffected by real-world regulation, and entirely independent of the financial system. The "stability" of stablecoins stems from the centralized structure behind them. It is precisely because of this centralized structure that they are stable, redeemable, widely circulated, and can be frozen by the issuer in cooperation with law enforcement. This isn't a simple matter of good or bad; it's an increasingly real structural problem within the industry. If you prioritize efficiency, liquidity, and dollar-denominated pricing, stablecoins certainly have their value. But if you desire complete censorship resistance, complete uncryability, and complete detachment from the real financial order, then stablecoins may not have been the answer from the start. Many people enjoy the convenience of stablecoins while simultaneously imagining them as decentralized assets like Bitcoin; this is a cognitive misalignment. For some sovereign nations, especially those seeking financial security, the practical value of this news may be even greater. Over the past few years, many countries have discussed how to reduce their dependence on the dollar system. Some countries hope to develop local currency settlements, some hope to promote central bank digital currencies, and some hope to use crypto assets to circumvent traditional financial sanctions. But if the final product is still a dollar-denominated stablecoin, it's essentially just replacing a dollar account with a dollar token. The form has changed, but the underlying power structure remains the same. You no longer use a US bank account, but you're using a dollar-denominated stablecoin within the US regulatory sphere. You no longer use SWIFT, but you're using a stablecoin issuer that cooperates with OFAC. You think you've moved from off-chain to on-chain, but US sanctions tools have also moved onto the blockchain with you. For a country truly pursuing financial security, this isn't a minor issue of technological routes, but a major issue of who controls the underlying financial infrastructure. Therefore, true financial security isn't simply about asking "whether it's on-chain," but rather about asking more fundamental questions: Who issued the assets, where are the reserves, who controls redemption, who influences compliance obligations, can the addresses be frozen, and are critical infrastructures controlled by others? Without clearly answering these questions, simply discussing "on-chain finance," "digital currency," and "stablecoin innovation" easily remains superficial. Of course, it's also wrong to say that stablecoins have no value because they can be frozen. That's too simplistic a judgment. The value of stablecoins lies precisely in their paradoxical nature. On one hand, they retain the efficiency of blockchain circulation; on the other hand, they retain the compliance interfaces of the real-world financial world. Because they are not fully decentralized assets, they are more easily accepted by institutions and more easily integrated into payment, clearing, cross-border trade, and financial service scenarios. But precisely because of this, they are destined not to be a purely "anti-sanctions tool." This is more like an upgrade of the dollar system under new technological conditions. In the past, dollars flowed through bank accounts and clearing systems; now, dollars can flow on public blockchains through stablecoins. In the past, the US exerted influence through banks, clearing houses, and SWIFT; now, the US can also exert influence through stablecoin issuers, centralized exchanges, on-chain analytics companies, and compliance service providers. Technology makes the dollar move faster, cover a wider area, and at a lower cost, but it hasn't removed the dollar from its original power structure. This isn't just ordinary sanctions news; it's a signal: global financial sanctions are entering the on-chain era. Previously, the US froze bank accounts; now, it freezes stablecoin addresses. Previously, those sanctioned worried about their dollar accounts being unusable; now, they'll also worry about their on-chain dollars being unusable. This is the most noteworthy aspect of the US freezing $344 million in Iranian cryptocurrency assets.

data.title: The Humanity Foundation adjusts the $H token Vesting scheme and sets a time limit.
data.description:
The Humanity Foundation recently made significant adjustments to its $H token vesting plan for investors. The vesting plan, originally scheduled to begin on June 25, 2026, and distributed over six quarters, is now subject to a final decision by April 26th at 09:00 UTC. Investors are required to respond to the email with one of two options: 1. Extend the vesting period, starting on September 25, 2026, with equal distribution over 12 quarters. 2. Immediate vesting with a 3:10 reduction ratio, replacing the original 16,666,666 $H with 5,000,000 $H (70% reduction), to be distributed in full on June 25, 2026. Currently, early-stage investor Trix Ventures has publicly disclosed choosing the discounted immediate vesting option. It is reported that the institution invested in the project at a valuation of approximately $60 million, and even after a 3:10 repurchase discount, it can still achieve a return of about 7 times. It is worth noting that Humanity Protocol previously had a deep partnership with payment giant Mastercard, giving the project fundamentals the backing of a traditional financial institution. The on-chain identity authentication sector, in which it operates, is currently in its early stages, but with the continued expansion of AI-generated content and automated accounts, the demand for on-chain real-name authentication is widely believed to be growing exponentially. This sector has the long-term potential to become a leading project in the Web3 infrastructure field. The project is about to face the test of a massive one-time unlock, and whether it can achieve explosive growth along with the AI ​​sector will be crucial.

I. 3:10 Instant Unlocking Ratio: The Only Rational Exit Currently

Although a 70% loss on paper is extremely heavy, in the current Web3 bear market cycle, "certain liquidity" far outweighs "illusory paper numbers," making monetization king. The extension plan extends the cycle to 3 years. In a volatile market, the protocol's survivability, technological iterations, and even team certainty after 3 years are huge unknowns. Professional investment institutions generally face pressure to recover funds from LPs; the 30% cash flow that is "secured" has a much higher turnover value than the 100% paper tokens that may go to zero after 3 years.

II. The "Death Spiral" Stress Test Will Appear on June 25, 2026

Choosing the 30% option is partly to recoup funds and secure a survival position before a potential "stampede" occurs.

A huge "sell-off wall": If 80% of investors choose to unlock on June 25, approximately 3.3% (330 million) of the total $H tokens will flood the market simultaneously. Institutional rush and hedging: After confirming the choice on April 26, there is still a two-month release window. Institutions are highly likely to hedge by shorting contracts or borrowing from DEXs.

This "selling before maturity" behavior will lead to some investors locking in huge profits through hedging before June 25th even arrives, potentially causing the price to halve before June 25th. On-chain hunting: Due to the use of Sablier contracts, the release plan is completely transparent. Quantitative teams and short-selling bots will precisely target the release node on June 25th. Locking in profits in advance by opening high-leverage short positions in the contract market will cause the price to face the risk of halving before the release.

III. Liquidity depletion: Defensive withdrawal of market makers (MMs)

Maintaining liquidity requires market makers. When the market anticipates a massive release of tokens (even at a discount) on June 25th, to prevent being overwhelmed by huge sell orders from investors on June 25th, market makers may choose to withdraw their buying depth in advance.

When investors attempt to sell 5,000,000 $H tokens on an exchange, significant slippage could result in the actual realized value being less than 10% of the original.

IV. Historical Lessons

Starknet (STRK): In February 2024, Starknet planned a large-scale unlocking just two months after its launch. The plan was deemed too aggressive and extremely unfriendly to the secondary market, sparking widespread condemnation and causing investor confidence to plummet. Its price fell by over 95% from its peak, becoming a typical "zombie L2." ApeCoin (APE): At the time, $APE also involved a large-scale investor unlocking, with the market starting to "rush" to sell about 30 days in advance. Even at the crucial vesting release point, the price still fell by 77% within 7 months.

Starknet (STRK): In February 2024, Starknet planned a large-scale unlocking just two months after its launch. The plan was deemed too aggressive and extremely unfriendly to the secondary market, sparking widespread condemnation and causing investor confidence to plummet. Its price fell by over 95% from its peak, becoming a typical "zombie L2."

ApeCoin (APE): At the time, $APE also involved a large-scale investor unlocking, with the market starting to sell off about 30 days in advance. Even at the crucial vesting release point, the price still fell by 77% within 7 months.

... If the Humanity Foundation unlocks 100% of the tokens to over 100 investors on June 25, 2026, this would be a "sell-off nuclear bomb" in the eyes of quantitative traders. Even if only 50% of the tokens are unlocked, the amount of tokens at that time will far exceed the market's buying depth. The valuation trap: the "discounted exit" cases of Vesta Finance (VSTA) and Hope.money are still fresh in our minds.

V. Decisively choosing a one-time unlock on June 25th is the wise choice

For early investors who have already profited, choosing the immediate unlocking plan on June 25th essentially means turning the tokens back into "free capital." It is understood that the Humanity Foundation has sent this letter to over 100 investors. In a game environment where everyone is worried about being too slow, being at the front of the release queue is the only hedging strategy.

data.title: A Rebuttal to Yang Haipo's "The End of Cryptocurrency"
data.description:
I've read Yang Haipo's "The Endgame of Cryptocurrency" carefully. Frankly, this isn't just a simple pessimistic view of the crypto market. Its real impact isn't its emotional tone, but its attempt to dissect the entire crypto industry into a simple cash flow statement: Bitcoin has no cash flow, the crypto industry consumes funds every year, miners, exchanges, project teams, KOLs, conferences, media, lawyers, and compliance companies all rely on this market for their livelihood, while the market itself lacks sufficient external revenue, ultimately relying on round after round of new money to stay afloat. When new money stops flowing in, the system will shrink, even collapse. This perspective and viewpoint are very sharp, and many points are not without merit. The crypto market does indeed have a large number of worthless projects, and there are indeed too many altcoins that rely on narratives to survive. Many so-called "revolutions," "future financial infrastructure," and "digital gold" ultimately boil down to a liquidity game between exchanges, project teams, VCs, market makers, KOLs, and retail investors. During bull markets, everyone feels they're participating in the future; only when the bear market arrives do they realize that this so-called future might just be a pre-planned exit strategy. This industry contains bubbles, leverage, Ponzi schemes, and gambling disguised as technical jargon. These things don't need whitewashing. Even many external critics don't see things as clearly as veterans in the crypto world themselves. Those who have truly experienced several cycles know that the cruelest aspect of the crypto world isn't price fluctuations, but its ability to constantly amplify human greed, wishful thinking, faith, and self-justification to the extreme. However, I disagree with the conclusion that everything is doomed simply because there are many bubbles in this industry. It proves that many projects are worthless, but it doesn't prove that all crypto assets are worthless; it proves that there is a lot of capital being wasted in the industry, but it doesn't prove that all of this wasted capital is meaningless; it proves that the crypto market has a strong speculative nature, but it doesn't prove that stablecoins, Bitcoin, on-chain settlement, and open financial infrastructure have no future. These issues need to be considered separately. I. Bitcoin and Cash Flow Bitcoin is certainly not a company, nor is it a business that generates cash flow. If you look at it like a company stock, it is indeed difficult to explain. Apple sells phones, Moutai sells liquor, banks earn interest spreads, and power companies sell electricity—these things can be calculated for revenue, profit, and cash flow, and valuation models can be built. But Bitcoin doesn't have these things. It doesn't hold board meetings, doesn't pay dividends, and doesn't issue an annual financial report to its holders. However, in this world, it's not only things that generate cash flow that have value. Gold doesn't generate cash flow. A gold bar sitting there won't magically create another. Artworks, collectibles, and certain scarce resources aren't essentially priced based on cash flow. Their value comes more from scarcity, verifiability, transferability, cultural consensus, and long-term reserve demand. The original text compares gold and Bitcoin, saying gold has jewelry, industrial, and sovereign reserve functions, while Bitcoin lacks these, therefore Bitcoin and gold cannot be compared. This comparison has its merits, but it easily overlooks one issue: the most valuable part of gold isn't its use in jewelry or industrial materials, but rather the reserve consensus it has formed over a long period, beyond sovereign credit. The real question to discuss isn't "Does Bitcoin have cash flow?", but "Can Bitcoin form a sufficiently stable consensus as a non-sovereign asset in the digital world?" This question, of course, has no standard answer. Supporters will argue that it is scarce, verifiable, transferable, self-custodial, and independent of any single country or financial institution; opponents will argue that it is too volatile, lacks sovereign credit backing, and has no real consumer demand. There are points for further discussion on both sides. However, it's a hasty conclusion to say it has no value simply because it lacks cash flow. More importantly, cash flow models are not the only way to evaluate all assets. Stocks can be evaluated based on profits, bonds on interest, and real estate on rental income, but monetary assets are often evaluated based on other factors: liquidity, credibility, scarcity, resistance to devaluation, and cross-institutional acceptance. II. Bitcoin's Resilience and Limitations Gold's advantage lies in its millennia-long history and lack of physical maintenance; a gold bar stored in a safe for a hundred years remains a gold bar. Bitcoin lacks this advantage. Bitcoin relies on the internet, electricity, miners, nodes, and software to operate; it is not a magical asset detached from the real world. However, the modern financial system also relies on infrastructure. Bank accounts rely on the banking system, securities trading relies on exchanges and clearinghouses, cross-border payments rely on banking networks and compliance systems, and mobile payments rely on communication networks, cloud services, and platform accounts. Today, almost no modern financial asset can exist completely independent of electricity, networks, and institutional systems. The difference lies in the fact that Bitcoin offers a different way of organization. It's not completely independent of infrastructure, but rather independent of any single centralized institution. Miners can migrate, nodes can be distributed, wallets can be self-custodied, and transactions can propagate through different paths. It is not indestructible, nor is it risk-free, but its vulnerability is not the same as the vulnerability of traditional centralized systems. Of course, Bitcoin's resilience should not be mythologized. It is not a panacea that works perfectly in environments of war, sanctions, internet outages, or power outages. Often, what ordinary people truly need isn't Bitcoin, but rather US dollars, cash, gold, personal networks, bank accounts, or a usable payment channel. However, Bitcoin at least offers an alternative to traditional financial accounts. The market will repeatedly price this option, but it cannot be said to be entirely meaningless. The original text also mentions the Bitcoin scaling debate, stating that the Core faction chose the small block route, actively abandoning payment functionality. From that moment on, Bitcoin degenerated from a "flawed currency" into a "pure consensus speculative commodity." This assessment is common in the BCH and BSV communities and indeed corresponds to a very important debate in Bitcoin's history. However, looking back, this matter is not so simple. Bitcoin's decision not to choose the large block size route was essentially a trade-off between two directions: one was to allow the base layer to handle more everyday payments, making Bitcoin more like a global payment network; the other was to keep the base layer as simple, conservative, low-cost, and verifiable as possible, allowing more ordinary nodes to participate in verification over the long term. The former pursued a better payment experience, while the latter pursued stronger base layer robustness. Different people will have different opinions, but it's hard to say this was simply a matter of "self-emasculation." It's a fact that Bitcoin has increasingly resembled a store of value asset rather than a high-frequency payment tool. But not everything in the financial system is responsible for buying coffee. Gold isn't responsible for everyday payments, central bank reserves aren't responsible for ordering takeout, and large-value clearing systems aren't designed for small street transactions. Whether an asset has value cannot be judged solely by whether it's suitable for everyday payments; it also depends on its function within the overall financial hierarchy. The real problem with Bitcoin today isn't that it hasn't become the global equivalent of Alipay, but whether it can become a sufficiently trustworthy, scarce, and censorship-resistant underlying asset in the digital world. IV. The Real Costs of the Industry The most influential part of the original article describes the entire crypto industry as a negative-sum system. Once money comes in, it's either consumed or remains in the system as margin. Miners need electricity, exchanges need personnel and compliance costs, project teams need salaries and marketing expenses, and KOLs, media, summits, lawyers, auditors, and market makers all need to make a living. The entire ecosystem incurs hundreds of billions of dollars in costs annually, and without a continuous influx of new funds, it will continuously bleed. This observation is very true. The crypto world is not a low-cost industry. It appears to be code and consensus, but behind it all are mining farms, electricity costs, servers, exchange teams, compliance licenses, customer service, market making, investment research, marketing, public relations, conferences, KOLs, lawyers, auditing, fund management fees, project team salaries, and the lifestyle consumption of numerous participants. During bull markets, these costs are masked by rising prices; only when a bear market arrives do people realize how heavy the industry's fixed expenses are. However, there's a problem here: we can't view all costs as deadweight losses. If we look at it that way, any emerging industry in its early stages resembles a negative-sum system. The early internet burned through massive amounts of server, bandwidth, personnel, office, and marketing expenses; the early new energy vehicle industry burned through massive amounts of R&D, subsidies, production capacity, and channel costs; the semiconductor industry has seen enormous long-term capital expenditures. We can't say an industry is worthless just because it has huge costs. The key is whether these costs have been channeled into infrastructure, user networks, technological capabilities, and business models. Of course, it can't be said that all the money was wasted. The electricity and equipment costs for miners at least covered the security budget of the Bitcoin network. Exchanges, wallets, custody, auditing, compliance, on-chain data, and stablecoin issuers do indeed constitute the infrastructure of this industry. V. Stablecoins: Undervalued Real Demand To elaborate further, the so-called "negative sum" certainly holds true at the transaction level. One person buys coins on the secondary market, another sells them, the exchange takes a commission, and the market maker earns the price difference. In the long run, all participants must bear the friction costs. The more frequent the short-term trading, the more obvious the overall loss. But the entire crypto industry is not just about secondary market trading. It also includes a series of different levels of activities such as issuance, custody, payment, clearing, lending, stablecoins, on-chain asset registration, and cross-border settlement. Stablecoins are a prime example. Many people, confined to the financial infrastructure of mainland China or the United States, find it difficult to understand why stablecoins are useful. This is because we are accustomed to bank accounts, bank cards, mobile payments, brokerage accounts, and various payment tools. However, this is not the case in many parts of the world. In many countries, it is not easy for ordinary people and small and medium-sized businesses to obtain dollar accounts, cross-border transfers are not cheap, banking systems are not widespread, and exchange rates fluctuate wildly. For someone engaged in small-scale cross-border trade, they may not care about decentralization or whether Bitcoin is digital gold; they only care about whether money can be received and sent out, whether transaction fees are high, how fast the funds arrive, and whether their account will be frozen unexpectedly. For these people, stablecoins are not a belief, but merely a tool. The truly interesting aspect of stablecoins is that, for the first time, a portion of dollar payments and settlements can flow with low barriers to entry on an open network. It's not about making everyone rich overnight, but about solving a very simple problem: can money flow more cheaply, faster, and more easily across accounts, platforms, and regions? VI. ETFs and DAT: Not the Last Infusion of Funds The original text says "the buyer list is exhausted," a judgment I also find somewhat static. Many people do know about Bitcoin today. Retail investors know, institutions know, exchanges have been doing it for over a decade, ETFs are listed, and mainstream financial institutions have researched it. The previous stage of attracting new users by saying "You don't know about Bitcoin yet?" is basically over. But knowing doesn't equal complete allocation. The asset market isn't calculated per capita, but rather based on asset allocation ratios, institutional constraints, product access, risk appetite, and the macroeconomic environment. Many institutions know about Bitcoin but haven't allocated any; many investment advisors can talk about Bitcoin but don't necessarily recommend it; many pension funds, insurance funds, sovereign wealth funds, and family offices, even if they are willing to allocate in the future, may only do so in very small proportions over a long period. ETFs have changed at least one thing: in the past, Bitcoin mainly existed on exchanges, wallets, and on-chain; now it has entered traditional brokerage accounts and asset management product systems. For many traditional investors, they don't want to register with cryptocurrency exchanges, don't want to manage their private keys themselves, and don't want to face the risks of on-chain transfers, but they can buy an ETF in their brokerage account. Whether this will lead to long-term allocation or just more convenient short-term trading remains to be seen. But at least it's not simply a matter of "buying the last wave of losses." It represents a change in asset entry points, a change in holding structure, and a reconnection between crypto assets and the traditional financial system. VII. Security Budget: A Real Issue, But Not a Death Sentence The concerns about Bitcoin's security budget in the original article are, in my opinion, the most serious part. With Bitcoin block rewards halving, future miners' income will rely more on transaction fees. If there isn't a sufficient transaction fee market in the long term, miners' income will decrease, hash rate will decrease, and attack costs will decrease, which theoretically will indeed affect network security. However, this issue cannot be categorized into a death spiral. A miner's security budget isn't just about block rewards; it also considers the price of Bitcoin, the transaction fee market, miner costs, power infrastructure, mining machine efficiency, financing capabilities, and the ability to migrate mining farms. A 51% attack is not a purely mathematical problem either. Attackers need to organize massive computing power, bear the risk of being identified and countered, and also face losses from the collapse of asset prices after the attack. A more prudent assessment would be: security budgets are a problem Bitcoin must face in the future, especially after multiple halvings, they will become increasingly important; however, it is not yet proof that Bitcoin is doomed. VIII. Pyramid Schemes: Not a One-Sided Statement The original text stated that the crypto industry is more efficient than pyramid schemes, a statement that is very persuasive and easily accepted by external readers. Many people observing the crypto world do indeed have this impression: a group of people attract newcomers through narratives, early investors profit from later ones, project teams and exchanges take a cut, and ultimately most people lose money and leave. This criticism is not without basis in reality. There are indeed many projects in the crypto industry that exhibit characteristics of Ponzi schemes and pyramid schemes. Many Ponzi schemes, commission-based schemes, pyramid schemes, pseudo-DeFi, and pseudo-RWA use technical jargon to package fund circulation, community consensus to lure new members, and decentralized slogans to evade responsibility. However, if we call all assets whose prices depend on later buyers pyramid schemes, then gold, art, collectibles, real estate, and growth stocks could all be called pyramid schemes during certain bubble phases. This sounds satisfying, but ultimately makes specific analysis impossible. What should truly be criticized is that many crypto projects use technical authenticity to mask economic hollowing out; they use decentralized narratives to evade project responsibility; and they use community consensus to pump and dump. This criticism should be as specific as possible, rather than condemning everything indiscriminately. IX. The Future of Industry Stratification Therefore, my attitude towards "The Endgame of Cryptocurrency" is rather complex. It makes some very good points. A large number of altcoins lack long-term value. Many project tokens are essentially a game of profit distribution between the team, VCs, exchanges, market makers, and retail investors. Early on, projects talk about technology; mid-term, they talk about the ecosystem; late-term, they talk about the community; and finally, it's just mutual encouragement in group chats. Many exchanges are indeed like casinos. What's more troublesome is that they don't openly admit they are casinos, but rather package high leverage as financial innovation, referral bonuses as community growth, and frequent user trading as market activity. Many KOLs are indeed liquidity hunters. In a bull market, they talk about faith; in a bear market, they talk about the long term; when distributing shares, they talk about the ecosystem; after a crash, they talk about knowledge. Retail investors think they are listening to industry analysis, but in reality, they are providing exit liquidity for others. The problem is, we can't say the entire city has no future just because there are cheaters in casinos; we can't say Bitcoin and stablecoins have no value just because most altcoins will go to zero. I'm more inclined to say that the crypto industry isn't without a future, but rather that it can no longer tell stories as cheaply as it used to. Previously, a project could launch a coin, write a white paper, find a few KOLs, and list on one or two exchanges to inflate its valuation. This phase will become increasingly difficult. What will survive in the future must answer more fundamental questions: Is anyone actually using it? Is anyone willing to pay for this service? Is there a real flow of funds, payments, and assets? Can it withstand regulation and cycles? This is actually similar to the early stages of many technology cycles. Around 2000, the dot-com bubble burst, many companies died, and many business models proved to be jokes. But the internet didn't die. What died was the bubble; what remained were the infrastructure and a few truly successful companies.

X. Conclusion

The greatest value of "The Endgame of Cryptocurrency" is reminding everyone not to be brainwashed by prices. A rising price does not equal established value, the launch of an ETF does not mean the risk has disappeared, and institutional entry does not mean ordinary people can blindly buy in.

But its problem is that it uses the industry's bubbles, casino-like practices, capital depletion, and altcoin collapses as a basis for judging the fate of the entire cryptocurrency industry.

I think a more realistic answer might be: the golden age of the crypto industry as a nationwide speculative casino will become increasingly difficult; the era of attracting newcomers with worthless coins, 100x returns, KOL endorsements, exchange rebates, and project promises will become increasingly fragile.

However, the crypto industry, as an early form of open financial infrastructure, has not yet reached its end. Many will die in the future, and few will remain. But few doesn't mean none. The problem with cryptocurrencies isn't whether there's an endgame, but who is qualified to survive to the next stage. Those that truly remain may no longer be the loudest, the fastest-rising, or the most captivating stories, but rather those that, after the bubble bursts, are still used, still paid for, and still able to solve problems in the gaps of the real financial system. This may be the true test for cryptocurrencies. It's not about whether prices reach new highs, or who will achieve financial freedom in the next bull market, but whether, after all the grand narratives have been washed away by the cycle, it can still leave behind some simpler, more durable, and more realistic values.

data.title: Contrarian Strategy: Predicting Market Exit Strategies
data.description:
Information determines liquidity

Before the World Cup, the world's biggest wave in the crypto industry will inevitably appear in the prediction market, but it's best to see this as an exit opportunity rather than the beginning of a massive surge.

This isn't just sensationalism. In last year's analysis of the prediction market, two basic consensuses were established:

  1. Where should the Chinese prediction market explore—the entertainment industry and the fan economy

  2. Where are the opportunities in the prediction market's peripheral service ecosystem in 2026?—Adding leverage to the prediction market

In subsequent practice, the prediction market as a whole has progressed along these two main lines and has begun to combine with emerging agent terminal forms such as Hermes, or leveraged players based on Hyperliquid such as HyperOdd.

However, prediction markets like Polymarket and Kalshi have significantly lower trading volumes than CEXs and other PERP products like Hyperliquid, and their ability to discover "price" is only about 60%, which suggests there's a problem. You can use prediction markets to predict the US president in advance, but you can't predict the future price movement of BTC. Survival Path Under Giants

"The World Cup is the last TGE window for copycat schemes."

The prediction market is in an absolute duopoly, with Kalshi/Polymarket swallowing up all liquidity. Kalshi's $22B and Polymarket's $15B valuations block all possibilities for newcomers.

If there was still a glimmer of hope before, then after Opinion, this window has been compressed to before the end of Q2. With the narrative of being Binance's favored child, coupled with the astonishing trading volume of $8B in 2026.1, Opinion successfully TGEed after exploiting users, and then immediately collapsed upon opening.

If nothing unexpected happens, CZ's handpicked...

... Predict Fun will likely follow suit. On Aster, CZ's buy orders were hesitant, but the $2 million buy was very effective. However, after repeated imitations, its impact on market sentiment gradually diminished. If we add Limitles for comparison, its FDV is only around $80M, half that of Opinion, and its price increase of 2.6x in the week following TGE was already its limit. This is completely incomparable to the competitive situation Hyperliquid faces with Binance. Polymarket and Kalshi have secured the top positions in the "niche market," which doesn't necessarily indicate their strength, but rather points to the upper limit of prediction markets. Firstly, prediction markets were the first narrative to emerge outside the Crypto-Native circle after the 2024 election, with valuations set by TradFi based on derivative asset classes, and Peter Thiel's Founders... Funds can publicly access Polymarket, but Hyperliquid is only publicly bought by Paradigm. The amount of capital available to either party depends on the size and maturity of the VC. Secondly, PL's valuation is lower than Kalshi's, which only suggests that the economic value of the compliant US market is greater than the global market. PL is already discounted, and its various chains, with their more aggressive counterparts, would have even higher risk discounts. After the Drift attack, the future of its embedded prediction market leveraged product, Drift BET, is uncertain; it will either end peacefully or undergo an airdrop to save itself, but the outcome is already predetermined. Replicating Polymarket's CLOB + binary options model on a different chain is feasible, but the discount would be extremely high. Besides this, there is also DeFi based on prediction markets. The model, as well as the two levels of segmentation and indexation, will be discussed in detail later.

    Generally, they can be divided into five types: from the lending layer that "makes use of dead money," to aggressive leverage, to passive market making by vaults, and the aggregation terminal and agent transaction layer. The following are representative projects:

    Lending Layer (Aave for Prediction Markets)

    • Gondor/Gondorfi (gondor.fi):
      Using Polymarket positions as collateral, with an LTV of 50%, and up to 75% in some markets, it supports immediate repayment and a fixed interest rate cap. For example, lenders can deposit USDC into Curated Funds, with a maximum APY of approximately 30%.

    • Joey's lending, created by @joeyroth, currently unnamed:
      It is under development, but is basically "fixed interest rate + no liquidation + no oracle dependence" to circumvent the UMA oracle controversy. The core mechanism is a leveraged lending protocol for PM positions (leveraging USDC, rather than traditional dynamic interest rate + liquidation).

      It is in the early development stage and may be a "CTF contract + Lending under the Yield mechanism" route, suitable for users with high leverage and long-term holding positions.

    Leverage layer (Perp DEX mode)

    • HyperOdd:
      Sports/sports/crypto/stock events support up to 20x leverage (up to 50x in some markets such as VIX), based on Hyperliquid liquidity.


    Currently online, offering Agentic Liquidity + Automated Market Making. Supports Polymarket data-derived perps, making it the most direct "20x no liquidation risk" player in the leverage layer.

    Vault Layer (Passive Market Making for Profits)

    • SIRE:
      Directly passively making markets on Kalshi, users deposit USDC as market-making funds, with profits flowing back to LPs.
      Following the official announcement of the partnership with Kalshi in September 2025, DeltaVault has been opened, allowing users to deposit funds to participate in AI-driven passive market making, primarily in markets such as sports.

    Vault Layer (Passive Market Making for Profits)

    ...>>>

    Aggregators and Trading Terminals

    • Polymtrade:
      Similar to products like GMGN from the Meme era, it supports multiple markets, multiple strategy trading, and arbitrage with one click.

    • Hermex (Hermes Agent × Predict.fun):
      Turns KOL tweets on Hermes into tradable prediction markets directly through a browser extension; the first consumer-grade Agent × PM product.

    • Fireplace:
      Completed a $1.5 million funding round in February 2026; the Pro version terminal is now online, targeting professional traders.

    • However, it should be noted that prediction market DeFi has not yet triggered secondary FOMO sentiment among capital and retail investors. PL/Kalshi can only increase liquidity around itself. In this case, prediction market DeFi faces the same situation as RWA. Simply entering the DeFi stack cannot generate liquidity. It is necessary to find a way to create buying sentiment among retail investors. Long-tail market liquidity. "The market function is not 'reflection' but 'discovery'." PL/Kalshi's liquidity has a cap, and there are liquidity barriers surrounding its DeFi ecosystem. Therefore, breaking down these liquidity barriers at the bottom level is necessary to enhance the market's price discovery capabilities. alt="Uj9gZFLTWJQcdYEQD5Y4v05oLND2KARuqAHnYQkL.png">

      As mentioned earlier, in terms of spatial structure, the prediction market is highly discretely distributed. Even top experts in various sectors can become novices in other markets, and retail investors will blindly follow their positions, leading to overall market inefficiency.

      There are highly differentiated specialized markets here, such as using AI's information discovery capabilities to make a single market $1B.

      The above-mentioned typical transactions, such as Sportstensor's use of large models + sports, engineer the discovery capabilities of various sports channels. Users don't need to understand historical top scorers; they only need to calculate betting ratios based on model parameters. The advantage of this is that it allows ordinary users to effectively participate in multiple markets without accumulating "tacit knowledge" of various tracks, improving market discovery efficiency. Since there is segmentation, aggregation is possible. However, it's not about aggregating funds, but rather aggregating the "wisdom of the crowd." Uninformed mass participation in the market only creates noise, but the judgments of the top players in each track can also be collectively financialized. The liquidity of the event market is naturally dispersed in silos, each with local tacit knowledge experts. What newcomers can do is not "open another new event market," but rather package the hundreds or thousands of events already traded on Kalshi/Polymarket into an index—creating a Crowd Wisdom. ETFs. This not only aggregates fund flows from multiple events, creating a scale effect that a single market cannot achieve, but also leverages individual analysts, allowing their expertise to be transmitted across different events through the index, preventing NFL experts and BTC analysts from tormenting each other in the same market. It aggregates tacit knowledge from various fields and automatically performs vertical weighted calculations based on their historical performance. Based on Polymarket's market size, there are 1,442 active markets under the Politics category. Current information is extremely fragmented; even experts in the political arena cannot understand every election. Even political index products possess theoretical potential. Conclusion: Prices are derived from imitation, and predictions often fulfill themselves. The size ceiling of prediction markets is embedded in their predictive effectiveness; an effective prediction market must defy consensus—if everyone knows the answer, there are no transactions. Prediction markets will never grow into entities like Hyperliquid/Binance, where "the higher the liquidity, the more valuable it is." But precisely because of this, they are always honest about their "future": the greater the uncertainty of the event, the greater its value in price discovery.

data.title: Aave receives pledge of 69,576 ETH; Musk's claim rejected.
data.description:
Headline

▌Aave has received 69,576 ETH in aid commitments, with only 5,505 ETH needed to cover the shortfall

On April 25th, Aave, as a lending platform directly affected by the rsETH incident, contributed 25,000 ETH ($57.75 million) to cover bad debts. The total bad debt shortfall was 75,081 ETH. Aave coordinated the establishment of the “DeFi United” program to receive aid funds to cover this shortfall, and has now received 69,576 ETH in aid commitments (loans and donations), leaving only a 5,505 ETH shortfall.

▌US Judge Rejects Elon Musk's Fraud Claims in OpenAI and Ultraman Lawsuits

On April 25, a US judge rejected Elon Musk's fraud claims in the OpenAI and Ultraman lawsuits. Musk's remaining claims will continue to proceed with the trial.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $77,480.47, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $2,317.16, up 0.4% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $636.08, up 0.6% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $86.26, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours. DOGE price is $0.09859, 24-hour change +1.5%; XRP price is $1.43, 24-hour change +0.0%; TRX price is $0.3230, 24-hour change +1.7%; WLFI price is $0.07582, 24-hour change +0.2%. HYPE price is $41.09, up +0.1% in 24 hours. Policy ▌US Department of Justice Stops Investigation of Federal Reserve, Possibly Paving the Way for Warsh's Senate Nomination On April 25, U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, Jeanine Piro, stated on the 24th that, given the Federal Reserve's anticipated internal investigation into cost overruns in its office building renovation project, she has instructed the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Columbia to end its investigation into the Fed's office building renovation project. According to U.S. media analysis, the Department of Justice's end of its criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Powell will pave the way for Senate approval of President Trump's nominee for the next Fed chairman, Kevin Warsh. Piro posted on social media that the Fed's inspector general was asked to review the multi-billion dollar construction cost overruns borne by taxpayers. She believes the investigation results will help "resolve the issue," and also stated that she would not hesitate to reopen a criminal investigation if necessary. (Xinhua News Agency) The total bad debt shortfall is 75,081 ETH. Aave coordinated the establishment of the "DeFi United" program to receive aid funds to fill the gap, and has now received aid commitments (loans and donations) of 69,576 ETH, leaving a shortfall of only 5,505 ETH. The White House stated on April 25th that the investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Powell is still ongoing. The case against the Federal Reserve is not necessarily to be dropped.

▌Tennessee Bans Crypto ATMs Statewide, Tightening Regulations to Combat Fraud

On April 25, Tennessee Governor Bill Lee signed a bill banning the operation of cryptocurrency ATMs statewide, making Tennessee the second state after Indiana to implement a complete ban. The bill (HB 2505) has officially taken effect and will be enforced on July 1. According to the regulations, installing or operating a "virtual currency self-service terminal" (i.e., a Bitcoin ATM) will be considered a Class A misdemeanor, punishable by up to one year in prison and a $2,500 fine. Merchants who allow such equipment to operate will also face legal liability. Currently, most states in the US have strengthened regulations through licensing systems and transaction limits, but complete bans remain rare. Data shows that since 2026, 30 states have proposed related legislation, with 20 of them having passed laws, demonstrating a continued tightening of regulations against cryptocurrency ATM fraud.

▌Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell's Term Nears End, Remaining on Board Remains Uncertain

On April 25, Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell may be in the final weeks of his central bank leadership career. Following the Justice Department's decision on Friday, he now faces a choice: whether to remain at the institution. U.S. Attorney Jeanne Piro announced on social media that she was transferring the criminal investigation into renovations at the Fed headquarters to the central bank's internal inspector general, effectively withdrawing the Justice Department from the investigation. Krishna Guha, Global Head of Policy and Central Bank Strategy at Evercore ISI, stated in a report: "Powell has remained tight-lipped. Without this investigation, we believe he would have left the Fed entirely on May 15. But we think the Justice Department's move may have come too late—and the threat of a reopened investigation is too uncertain—for Powell to leave on May 15." Guha speculates that, instead, Powell may remain in office for some time, even if he does not fully complete his term as a board member, which expires in January 2028.

▌US Judge Questions Trump's $10 Billion IRS Leaks Lawsuit

On April 25, a judge questioned whether President Trump's $10 billion privacy lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) and the US Treasury Department could proceed, given that Trump effectively controls both sides. US District Judge Kathleen Williams ordered the Justice Department to provide a written explanation by May 20 regarding how she would oversee the case when the Constitution requires courts to have legitimate "adversarial" jurisdiction. The judge stated that, given Trump's incumbent presidency and the fact that his opponents are entities under his leadership, "this court is currently unsure whether the parties are sufficiently adversarial" to establish jurisdiction.

▌CFTC Files Amicus Brief with Massachusetts Supreme Court to Defend Federal Regulatory Authority on Prediction Markets

On April 25, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) filed an amicus brief with the Massachusetts Supreme Court, reaffirming its exclusive jurisdiction over the U.S. commodity derivatives market (including prediction markets) in the case of Commonwealth of Massachusetts v. KalshiEx LLC (SJC-13906). CFTC Chairman Michael S. Selig stated that Congress has authorized the CFTC to have sole regulatory authority over the commodity derivatives market and warned any state attempting to override federal law "to see them in court." The brief details the legislative history and framework of the Commodity Exchange Act, clarifying that the federal regulatory system takes precedence over relevant state laws.

Previously, the CFTC had filed lawsuits against Arizona, Connecticut, Illinois, and New York, and successfully obtained a temporary restraining order against Arizona, preventing it from exercising state jurisdiction over the prediction markets regulated by the CFTC.

▌Trump's lawsuit against the Treasury and IRS faces court challenges

On April 25, local time, on April 24, a lawsuit filed by US President Donald Trump against the IRS and Treasury Department was challenged by the court. US District Judge Catherine Williams pointed out that given Trump's actual influence over both sides, the case's "genuine adversarial contention" was questionable, and requested the Department of Justice to submit a written explanation by May 20 regarding how the court had jurisdiction over the case. (Jinshi)

    Blockchain Applications

    ▌NYSE Arca Applies to List and Trade "T. Rowe Price Active Crypto ETF"

    On April 25, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) announced that Arca, a subsidiary of the New York Stock Exchange, has proposed to amend its rules to list and trade the "T. Rowe Price Active Crypto ETF". The fund will primarily invest in a diversified basket of 5 to 15 crypto assets, including Bitcoin and Ethereum, and will hold the USDC stablecoin as tokenized cash, aiming to achieve long-term capital growth through an active strategy. The SEC is currently soliciting public comments on this proposal.

    Cryptocurrency

    ▌US Freezes $344 Million in Iran-Related Cryptocurrencies

    On April 25, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated that the Treasury Department is sanctioning multiple wallets linked to Iran and will track and strike all financial lifelines associated with the regime. Tether previously announced that it had assisted the US government in freezing $344 million worth of cryptocurrency in two addresses. Chainalysis data shows that the activity patterns of these wallets are consistent with the on-chain fund flow characteristics of wallets belonging to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. US officials stated that blockchain analysis revealed that the funds were transacted through intermediary addresses with wallets linked to the Central Bank of Iran to circumvent sanctions and facilitate international trade. Iran's cryptocurrency holdings are projected to reach $7.8 billion by 2025, with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps holding approximately 50% of that amount in the fourth quarter of last year.

    Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF Adds Approximately 120 Bitcoins, Total Holdings Reach 1834 Bitcoins On April 25th, according to Arkham monitoring data, the on-chain wallet address of Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust (MSBT), a spot Bitcoin exchange-traded fund, added 120.413 BTC, worth $9.35 million, five hours ago. As of now, MSBT's total Bitcoin holdings have reached 1834, worth approximately $142 million. Bitmine Pledges Another 112,040 ETH, Worth Over $260 Million On April 25th, according to OnchainLens monitoring, Bitmine pledged another 112,040 ETH, worth over $260 million, four hours ago. **Important Economic Developments** On April 25, following chipmaker Intel's financial outlook indicating a sales recovery, the value of the U.S. government's stake in Intel (INTC.O) surged 300% to approximately $36 billion. This represents a paper return of nearly $27 billion since Intel and the U.S. government announced the investment last August. The company's CEO won the White House's support through a charm offensive and repaired his relationship with President Trump, ultimately securing this extraordinary investment.

    ▌Nasdaq and S&P 500 close at new record highs

    On April 25, the three major U.S. stock indexes closed with divergent trends. The Nasdaq rose 1.63%, and the S&P 500 rose 0.8%, both hitting new record highs. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.16%. Popular technology stocks generally rose, with Intel up over 23%, Nvidia up over 4%, Amazon up over 3%, Microsoft and Meta up over 2%, and Google up over 1%. Semiconductor, computer hardware, and storage stocks led the gains, with Arm up over 14%, AMD up over 13%, Qualcomm up over 11%, AMD up over 8%, and Micron Technology up over 3%.

    ...

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

    Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

    Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

    Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: The Nasdaq hits a new high. Who is really driving this rally?
data.description:
Author: 137Labs

Recently, the US technology stock market, represented by the Nasdaq Composite Index, has once again refreshed its intraday historical high. At the same time, cryptocurrency concept stocks have shown significant strength. From the market's surface appearance, this is a typical technology stock-driven rally. However, by analyzing the weighting structure, volatility indicators, and sector rotation rhythm, it can be found that this round of rise is actually the result of a triple mechanism: "concentration of weighting + strengthening of profit expectations + release of risk appetite."

From Panic to Recovery: A Sentiment Quantification Path Based on VIX

Over the past month, the most fundamental change in the market has not been the price itself, but rather the reconstruction of the risk pricing mechanism. Sentiment indicators, represented by the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), provide the most direct quantitative evidence.

By comparing VIX changes with sector performance, a clear risk transmission model can be constructed:

·When VIX > 30: Funds concentrate on defensive assets

·When VIX falls to 25–20: Funds flow back to leading technology stocks

·When VIX < 20: Funds spread to semiconductors and high-beta assets

This logic has been fully validated over the past month. As the VIX fell from 31 to 18, the market experienced a complete process from defense to offense and then to diffusion, with the sector rotation order being: AI leaders → Semiconductors → Cryptocurrency concept stocks.

**Stage Positioning: Characteristics of a Mid-to-Late Stage of a Broadening Market** Combining weighting structure, earnings data, and volatility indicators, the current market can be clearly positioned as a "mid-to-late stage of broadening." This stage typically exhibits the following characteristics: First, the index continues to reach new highs, but the gains are concentrated in a few heavyweight stocks; second, sector rotation accelerates, and high-beta assets become active; finally, volatility remains low, but potential volatility risks are accumulating. Historically, when the VIX remains below 20 for an extended period and the index continues to reach new highs, the market often enters a "sentiment-driven zone," where prices become significantly more sensitive to expectations. **Potential Risks and Forward-Looking Assessments** Despite the current strong market performance, several key variables still require attention from a data perspective. First, there's the valuation level. The technology sector is already at a historically high level due to high growth expectations. If earnings fall short of expectations, it could trigger a pullback in heavyweight stocks. Second, there's volatility. Once the VIX rises above 22 again, it usually indicates a contraction in risk appetite. Finally, there's the sector structure. If core semiconductor or AI companies show a trend of weakness, it will directly affect the index performance. In conclusion, this round of Nasdaq record highs is a typical structural market trend, driven primarily by the expansion of the technology sector's weighting and earnings growth, coupled with a sustained recovery in risk appetite. From the rapid decline of the VIX to the subsequent rise in the semiconductor and crypto sectors, the market has completed a full risk pricing restructuring process. In this context, understanding the dynamic relationship between "weighting, earnings, and sentiment" will be key to judging the future market direction.
